channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Rhode Island Man Who Fled 2005 Rape Trial Is Arrested in New York
item.description: Ronald L. Fischer vanished just before he was convicted. After more than 20 years on the run, he was arrested on a sailboat in the East River, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald

item.title: How Trump Is Using Federal Agencies to Target Elections
item.description: Nick Corasaniti, a Politics reporter, explains how the Trump administration is deploying federal agencies to investigate and reshape electoral processes under state jurisdiction.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Corasaniti, Laura Bult, Nour Idriss, June Kim and Stephanie Swart

item.title: U.S. Forest Service Employees Are Freed After 15-Hour Hostage Ordeal
item.description: The authorities said a man and his son held two employees at gunpoint in a trailer at Shasta-Trinity National Forest in Northern California.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai

item.title: Mourning One Tragedy at Sea, a Family Is Devastated by Another
item.description: The Boisas went out on the San Francisco Bay to spread the ashes of one of their own. Then a wave toppled their boat.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla, Madaleine Rubin and Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Stephen Lam/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Alcatraz Island a day after a boat named Volare capsized in rough seas near the island.

item.title: AIPAC Closes Donor Portal to Democrats Who Voted to End Aid to Israel
item.description: The pro-Israel lobbying group appeared to be punishing Democrats it had endorsed for re-election after they voted in favor of an amendment that would have cut off U.S. aid to Israel.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: In AIPAC’s web portal, the option of donating to House Democrats who voted to end U.S. aid to Israel was unavailable. That included Representative Katherine Clark of Massachusetts, left.

item.title: South Carolina Man Who Fired Rifle to Scare a Black Neighbor Gets 9 Years
item.description: The man, Jonathan Andrew Felkel, fired a rifle and shouted a racial slur at the victim, whom he targeted because of his race, law enforcement officials said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: Paul Pelosi Charged With Hit-and-Run in Napa Valley Crash
item.description: Mr. Pelosi, the 86-year-old husband of former Speaker Nancy Pelosi, crashed into a parked Tesla near his home among the vineyards this month.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Pelosi crashed his convertible into a parked Tesla and continued to drive down a street in Yountville, Calif., on July 3.

item.title: Billionaires Prepare $87 Million Ad Campaign to Block California Wealth Tax
item.description: A coalition backed by the Google co-founder Sergey Brin and wealthy tech investors is trying to neutralize a proposed one-time tax on the state’s richest residents.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Laurel Rosenhall
item.category: Service Employees International Union-United Healthcare Workers West
item.media:credit: Jae C. Hong/Associated Press
item.media:description: A campaign event for a proposed billionaire tax in Los Angeles in February.

item.title: Trump Endorses Graham for Senate, Scrambling South Carolina Primary
item.description: Senator Darline Graham had been seen as a caretaker for her brother’s seat after his sudden death. Now she has the president’s nod in what was shaping up to be a crowded fight to succeed Lindsey Graham.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Darline Graham, left, was sworn in on Tuesday at the Capitol.

item.title: Who Really Has the 2026 Midterms Cash Edge?
item.description: The fund-raising race is tighter than it seems.
item.dc:creator: Jess Bidgood
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Ken Paxton last month at the 2026 Republican Party of Texas convention in Houston. Mr. Paxton, the party’s nominee for Senate, has been drastically out-raised by his Democratic rival, James Talarico.

item.title: Trump Pursues a Deeper Bond With China’s Leader, Despite Hostile Speech
item.description: Chinese officials appear to think that President Trump’s accusations that China interfered in the 2020 U.S. elections were driven by domestic politics, not foreign policy.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Xi Jinping and President Trump participated in events at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing in May.

item.title: Sara Rodriguez, Lieutenant Governor, Quits Wisconsin Governor’s Race
item.description: One establishment candidate dropped out and another rejoined the race amid worries from some moderate Democrats that a democratic socialist might win the primary.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman and Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez’s withdrawal upended the race in Wisconsin.

item.title: Trump’s Election Claims and SAVE Act Push Find Muted Response From G.O.P. Lawmakers
item.description: Most G.O.P. lawmakers had little to say about the president’s claims of election vulnerabilities, and he did not appear to move the needle on the voting restriction bill he championed.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold, Carl Hulse, Reid J. Epstein and Erik Wemple
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, seen on a screen during his speech at the White House on Thursday. Mr. Trump has made baseless assertions of election fraud for years, largely pointing to a similar set of concerns.

item.title: Trump’s Homeland Security Chief Threatens Election Officials With Prison Time
item.description: Markwayne Mullin reiterated the president’s false claims about voting security while escalating the administration’s legally questionable attempts to control state elections.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Markwayne Mullin, the secretary of homeland security, repeated several false claims made by President Trump in a prime-time speech Thursday about the security of U.S. elections.

item.title: Man Sought in Minnesota Fraud Case Returned From Somalia
item.description: Abdikerm Eidleh, whom the authorities sought for nearly four years, faces charges in the state’s sprawling social services fraud case, which has drawn the Trump administration’s attention.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abdikerm Eidleh was among dozens of people federal prosecutors have charged in connection with a sprawling fraud case.

item.title: Mullin Vows to Keep Up Immigration Arrests Even After ICE Shootings
item.description: The homeland security secretary made clear that he would seek to deliver on President Trump’s push for mass deportations.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, speaking in Washington on Friday.

item.title: Deadly Texas Floods Ebb, but Danger Remains: ‘You Could Hear the Water’
item.description: At least two people died as waters rose rapidly across the Texas Hill Country, the same area of the state that saw catastrophic flooding last year.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy, Ramón Ramirez and J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Debris littered a bridge crossing the Guadalupe River in Center Point, Texas.

item.title: Trump Faces the Limits of U.S. Firepower and the Lessons of Past Wars
item.description: Like his predecessors, President Trump has struggled to turn battlefield successes into long-term victories.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We’re going to knock out all their power plants,” President Trump told Fox News this week. “We’re going to knock out all their bridges unless they get to the table and negotiate.”

item.title: Remains of California Millionaire Who Vanished 45 Years Ago Are Identified
item.description: A con man was convicted in the 1983 murder of Thelma Gaston, 80, but her remains were only just identified thanks to advanced DNA testing.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Alamy Stock Photo
item.media:description: Remains found in 1981 in the Santa Rosa Mountains near Palm Springs, Calif., were recently identified as those of Thelma Gaston, who was 80 when she was reported missing.

item.title: Trump Brings Full Weight of Government to Bolster False Election Claims
item.description: Intelligence agencies provided the White House with a trove of declassified documents that President Trump cited on Thursday as evidence of election vulnerabilities.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Nick Corasaniti and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times

item.title: Judges Ask Congress for Power to Fix Crumbling Courthouses
item.description: Undrinkable water. Leaks. Mold. Federal courthouses need billions of dollars in repairs, and judges say the General Services Administration is a bad landlord.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inside the modernist, steel-and-glass exterior of the Everett McKinley Dirksen U.S. Courthouse in Chicago, judges say that longstanding maintenance problems are interfering with the performance of their duties.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones, Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Campbell Robertson

item.title: Trump Promised Proof of Election Tampering. His Document Release Fell Far Short.
item.description: In the end, the documentary evidence that President Trump claimed would prove his case appeared bound to disappoint those who expected bombshell revelations.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Saul Loeb
item.media:description: President Donald Trump points to attendees after delivering an address to the nation from the East Room on Thursday night.

item.title: How ICE’s Traffic Stops Led to Fatal Confrontations
item.description: ICE has been trying to continue its mass deportations without drawing headlines. Our White House correspondent Zolan Kanno-Youngs explains how two fatal shootings at traffic stops raise the question of whether the Trump administration can continue its campaign without deadly consequences.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Gilad Thaler, Edward Vega, Jon Miller, Paul Abowd, Stephanie Swart and Rafaela Balster

item.title: In One of Houston’s Oldest Latino Barrios, Mourning After ICE Shooting Mixed With Fear and Anger
item.description: After a federal agent killed Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, the grief and anger in Magnolia Park has been less visible, but no less intense.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of people prayed together at a memorial for Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston on Saturday.

item.title: Republicans in North Carolina Try to Reduce Early Voting on Sundays and on Campuses
item.description: Voting-rights activists said the changes are a blatant attempt by G.O.P. leaders to make it harder for Black voters and students, who tend to vote for Democrats, to cast ballots this fall.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Jonathan Drake/Reuters
item.media:description: Republican changes to early voting locations in North Carolina have prompted more than a dozen disputes between state and local election officials.

item.title: To Win Their Races, Black Politicians Confront a New Landscape
item.description: With the Voting Rights Act weakened, Black representation will depend less on Black voters and more on broad, multiracial appeal or on ideological outsider campaigns.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam and Clyde McGrady
item.media:credit: Mustafa Hussain for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The idea that Black candidates are only electable in Black districts is patently false,” said Representative Lauren Underwood, who represents a largely white district.
